Course Title: Smart Factories and AI Attack Surfaces
Category: Manufacturing: Chemicals, Food Manufacturing, Industrial Manufacturing, Textiles, Automotive, Pharmaceuticals, Electronics
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic familiarity with manufacturing environments and a general understanding of what the internet is, though no technical or cybersecurity background is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key ways in which increased connectivity in smart factories expands the attack surface for cyber threats, and explain how AI-driven systems introduce new vulnerabilities across manufacturing sectors such as chemicals, pharmaceuticals, and automotive production. You will also be able to apply foundational risk awareness principles to recognise potential weak points in connected industrial environments, helping you take a more informed and proactive approach to cybersecurity in your workplace.

lessons.lesson title: Smart Factories and AI Attack Surfaces
lessons.Description: Smart factories rely on interconnected machines, sensors, and software systems that communicate constantly, and this connectivity, whilst powerful, creates new opportunities for attackers to exploit. This lesson introduces the concept of attack surfaces and explains how adding more connected devices and AI-driven systems to a factory environment can expand the number of potential entry points for cyber threats. You will explore how traditional industrial equipment, once isolated from external networks, now faces risks that were previously associated only with office IT systems. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of why securing a smart factory requires thinking beyond conventional cybersecurity approaches and considering the unique vulnerabilities that AI and automation introduce.

slides.subtitle: Where connectivity meets manufacturing today.
slides.slidecontent:
• Factories now run on networks
• Machines talk to each other
• Data flows constantly
• More connections, more risk
• This affects every manufacturer

slides.subtitle: Understanding your digital doorways.
slides.slidecontent:
• Attack surface = all possible entry points
• Every connected device creates a new pathway
• One compromised component threatens the whole network
• Legacy equipment wasn't built for security
• Remote access multiplies exposure points

slides.subtitle: When sensors become security risks.
slides.slidecontent:
• Sensors, cameras, controllers all vulnerable
• Default passwords rarely changed
• Security patches often delayed or unavailable
• Devices run unmonitored for years
• Each device multiplies attack opportunities

slides.subtitle: How artificial intelligence becomes a target.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI models can be poisoned with bad data
• Machine learning algorithms make biased decisions
• Training data reveals sensitive information
• AI systems lack traditional security controls
• Automated decisions spread compromise quickly

slides.subtitle: When old machines join modern networks.
slides.slidecontent:
• Equipment from 1980s-90s lacks security design
• No encryption or authentication built-in
• Vendors no longer provide support or patches
• Critical systems can't be updated without downtime
• One old component compromises entire network

slides.subtitle: How industrial networks expose data.
slides.slidecontent:
• Legacy protocols ignore authentication
• Unencrypted data travels network freely
• Known vulnerabilities remain unpatched
• Devices trust any received instructions
• Attackers exploit documented weaknesses

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to minimize exposure.
slides.slidecontent:
• Segment networks to limit attacker movement
• Apply strict access controls on AI systems
• Audit data collection and sharing regularly
• Patch connected devices consistently
• Train staff to recognize unusual behavior

slides.subtitle: Essential security principles to remember.
slides.slidecontent:
• Connectivity and risk always grow together
• AI systems face unique manipulation threats
• Network segmentation contains breaches
• Regular audits reveal hidden connections
• Start small: map one system this week

scripts.script: Welcome to the course. Let's start by setting the scene. If you walked into a modern factory today, you might be surprised by what you saw. It would not just be machines and workers. It would be a living, breathing network. Machines on the factory floor are constantly communicating with each other, sharing data, adjusting processes, and reporting back to central systems, often in real time. That constant flow of information is what makes modern manufacturing so efficient and responsive. But here is the thing: every connection in that network is also a potential doorway. The more devices and systems you link together, the more opportunities there are for something to go wrong, whether through a technical fault or, increasingly, a deliberate attack. And this is not just a concern for large corporations. Manufacturers of every size are affected.

Let's get clear on what we mean by attack surface. Think of it as all the possible ways an attacker could try to get into your systems. In a traditional factory, that might have been just one computer connected to the internet. But in a smart factory, every sensor, every controller, every piece of connected equipment is essentially a potential entry point. What makes this particularly dangerous is that these systems are all linked together. So if an attacker finds their way in through one vulnerable device, they can potentially access the entire production network from there. And here's the challenge: much of this equipment was never designed with network security as a priority. It was built to do a job efficiently, not to defend against cyber attacks.

Now let's talk about the devices themselves. In a smart factory, you might have hundreds or even thousands of sensors, cameras, and controllers, all collecting data and communicating across the network. Each one of these devices represents a potential security risk. Many ship with default usernames and passwords that are never changed. Security updates are often delayed or simply not available for older equipment. And because these devices are designed to run autonomously, they can operate for years without anyone checking whether they've been compromised. What makes this particularly concerning is that attackers actively look for these weak points. They scan networks specifically looking for devices with known vulnerabilities or default settings that haven't been secured.

AI systems bring their own unique set of vulnerabilities to the factory floor. Unlike traditional software that follows predictable rules, AI models learn from data, and that learning process can be manipulated. Attackers can poison the training data, causing the AI to make incorrect decisions that could disrupt production or compromise quality. Machine learning algorithms can also be tricked into making biased decisions by feeding them carefully crafted input data. What's particularly concerning is that AI systems often have access to vast amounts of sensitive operational data, and they can make automated decisions that spread throughout the production process very quickly. This means that when an AI system is compromised, the impact can be both immediate and widespread.

One of the most surprising vulnerabilities in smart factories comes from legacy equipment that's been running reliably for decades. We're talking about machines from the 1980s and 1990s that were never designed to be connected to networks. These systems have no built-in security features like encryption or authentication. When you connect this old equipment to modern networks, you create serious security gaps. The problem is compounded by the fact that updating these machines is often impossible without significant downtime, which factories simply cannot afford. So known vulnerabilities remain unpatched, creating persistent weak points that attackers can exploit to gain access to the entire connected system.

The way industrial systems communicate also creates significant risks. Many legacy industrial protocols were designed for closed networks where security wasn't a concern. These protocols often have no authentication mechanisms, meaning any device on the network can send commands to any other device. Data frequently travels across these networks completely unencrypted, so anyone who gains network access can read sensitive operational information. Attackers are well aware of these weaknesses because many industrial protocol vulnerabilities are publicly documented. This means they can target specific known weaknesses in systems that may never receive security updates.

So what can you actually do to reduce these risks? Start with network segmentation - keep your operational technology separated from your IT systems wherever possible. This limits how far an attacker can move if they do get in. For AI systems handling production data, implement strict access controls and regularly review who has permission to access what. Conduct regular audits to understand what data your systems are collecting and sharing - you might be surprised by what you discover. Keep all connected devices patched and updated, including the ones that are easy to forget about. And invest in training your staff to recognize when systems aren't behaving normally, because human awareness often catches problems that automated systems miss.

Let's pull together the key lessons from this course. First, connectivity and risk always move together - the more connected your factory becomes, the larger your attack surface grows. AI systems face unique threats beyond traditional network attacks, including data poisoning and algorithmic manipulation. Network segmentation is one of your most effective defenses because it contains breaches and limits attacker movement. Regular audits help you understand your real exposure by mapping out connections you might not have realized existed. And here's something practical you can start with immediately: choose one system this week and document everything it connects to. That simple exercise will give you valuable insights into your actual risk exposure and help you prioritize your security efforts.

Who is this for: Manufacturing supervisors, plant managers, production managers, operations managers, facility managers, quality control managers, maintenance supervisors, industrial engineers, process engineers, manufacturing technicians
Team or Dept this is for: Operations, Manufacturing, Production, Engineering, Quality Assurance, Maintenance, Plant Management
